I have a question. What is the hierachy of rustcode in linux?
I mean are they all in a giant crate or every module is in a standalone crate??
One crate per kernel module. Note, however, that rust/kernel/ is one "giant crate", and it is shared.

Thus, at the moment, the hierarchy is very simple, only 2 layers: at the top you have libkernel which has all the Rust support and abstractions, and then every single other module written in Rust depends on that one only.

So From an application perspective, libkernel works like a "standard library"
But from memory layout, it's a part of kernel, not a independent library.
Is it correct??
Not sure what you mean -- the shared Rust code gets linked into the kernel image like any other C builtin object file.

Thank you!!
You're welcome! :)

another question:
image.png
In this situation, should I put an INVARIANT block here??
Can you please post the code as text?

OK

#[allow(dead_code)]
pub(crate) fn from_kernel_err_ptr<T>(ptr: mut T) -> Result<mut T> {
extern "C" {
#[allow(improper_ctypes)]
fn rust_helper_is_err(ptr: *const c_types::c_void) -> bool;

    #[allow(improper_ctypes)]
    fn rust_helper_ptr_err(ptr: *const c_types::c_void) -> c_types::c_long;
}

// CAST: casting a pointer to `*const c_types::c_void` is always valid.
let const_ptr: *const c_types::c_void = ptr.cast();
// SAFETY: the FFI function does not deref the pointer.
if unsafe { rust_helper_is_err(const_ptr) } {
    // SAFETY: the FFI function does not deref the pointer.
    let err = unsafe { rust_helper_ptr_err(const_ptr) };
    // CAST: if `rust_helper_is_err()` returns `true`,
    // then `rust_helper_ptr_err()` is guaranteed to return a
    // negative value greater-or-equal to `-bindings::MAX_ERRNO`,
    // which always fits in an `i16`, as per the invariant above.
    // And an `i16` always fits in an `i32`. So casting `err` to
    // an `i32` can never overflow, and is always valid.
    //
    // INVARIANT: `rust_helper_is_err()` ensures `err` is a              <---------- here
    // negative value greater-or-equal to `-bindings::MAX_ERRNO`
    return Err(unsafe { Error::from_kernel_errno_unchecked(err as i32) });
}
Ok(ptr)

}
No, INVARIANT should only be used in the implementation of methods of types that have an invariant.
Please use SAFETY for unsafe blocks.

Hi everyone! Thank you for this great and exciting effort. I haven't done any kernel module development for long time, so I apologise if my questions are too naive. As far as I understand, the current https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux aims to be for (future) "in-tree" development against the latest 5.12+ kernel. I'd like to work on an "out-of-tree" module against a kernel in the current common LTS distros (e.g. 5.8.0) -- in C, one can set up a simple makefile for e.g. make -C /lib/modules/uname -r/build ... which won't directly work here (as that old kernel build dir will be missing all the Rust-related stuff)... What setup would you recommend for this case? I saw https://github.com/fishinabarrel/linux-kernel-module-rust but that repo is archived and points to Rust-for-Linux + that repo uses cargo... which Rust-for-Linux seemed to have moved away from and uses rustc instead directly? Does anyone know of any other repos / good templates for the out of tree module development setup?

Tomas Tauber said:

As far as I understand, the current https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux aims to be for (future) "in-tree" development against the latest 5.12+ kernel.

Correct -- we will attempt to get mainlined for 5.14 (next merge window), but as usual, depends on feedback.

Tomas Tauber said:

I'd like to work on an "out-of-tree" module against a kernel in the current common LTS distros (e.g. 5.8.0) -- in C, one can set up a simple makefile for e.g. make -C /lib/modules/uname -r/build ... which won't directly work here (as that old kernel build dir will be missing all the Rust-related stuff)... What setup would you recommend for this case?

If you really want to work on out-of-tree modules, then yeah, you will have to backport everything. Is there any particular reason why you want to work on older kernel releases?

In any case, note that the kernel in general does not do feature backporting, and we are focused on getting into mainline first. After that, if the kernel community really wanted to see Rust support for older releases, we could work on that, but it is not the focus at all at the present time.

Miguel Ojeda said:

If you really want to work on out-of-tree modules, then yeah, you will have to backport everything. Is there any particular reason why you want to work on older kernel releases?

To have the module working in the current common distros which bundle older kernel releases.

Tomas Tauber said:

To have the module working in the current common distros which bundle older kernel releases.

I see -- if the module is for a company and/or you have enough time/resources, then I would suggest the best approach right now is to write both the C version of the driver + the Rust version.

That way you get several advantages:

  • Best support for old releases.
  • A baseline for comparison with the Rust version, e.g. benchmarking etc.
  • Having your abstractions ready by the time Rust is a thing in the kernel.

The line I run is:
"bindgen /home/jose/Documents/Rust-For-Linux/linux/include/asm-generic/rwonce.h -o rwonce_rust.rs -- -I/home/jose/Documents/Rust-For-Linux/linux/include"
Oh no, sorry, that's not how I did it. I added #include "asm/rwonce.h" to rust/kernel/bindings_helper.h` and recompiled.

Yeah, as @Arthur Cohen explains, you should not generate bindings on your own ("offline"). Instead, put what you need in bindings_helper.h and the build system will take care of generating them appropiately.

Even then, it could happen that you run into some small issues with new headers that we have not tested -- sometimes we need to mark a type as opaque, etc. If you hit such an issue, let us know and we can see how we can make it work and/or submit the bug to the bindgen folks :)

To clarify even further: the policy we are following is that nobody except rust/kernel/ code should call the C side, and thus should never need bindings. This means that if you are writing a driver in Rust, you should first provide the abstractions you need in the shared code -- and then make the driver use that; instead of bypassing the shared infrastructure.

bindgen cannot bind inline functions and macros. Unfortunately, kernel module takes a lot of such things. If you want to write a Rust module from scratch, all these macros and inline functions must be properly placed in wrapper helpers.

Is there any best practice about "binding" inline functions from C? For now, we use helper functions as wrappers to these inlines. However, with the growth of Rust in the kernel, more and more helpers will be finally added. Also, the helper function adds extra penalty on performance compared to the bare C calls.
Currently, all helper functions are placed in rust/helpers.c. I think it is good to use a stand-alone directory to store these helpers according to the subsystem/functionality.
Yes, we will have to see how to scale it better. There is a PR open about helpers too, in case you want to take a look.

Hello Rust for Linux! Newbie here. I wanted to write a module that determined whether it was running within a process context (rather than interrupt context) and grabbed its credentials (uid, gid, euid, etc.). In the C version of the module, I've used the related functions in_task(), current_uid(), current_euid(), etc. I don't see these in the generated bindings so is my approach in adding wrapper functions for these in helpers.c the right one?

On a more broader pattern, I'm struggling to know when to defer to writing a helper that wraps the C equivalent (and therefore shades us from the implementation) or bringing in the implementation details into the Rust side. As a simple example, we currently, in Task::pid() look at a task_struct's pid field. On the C side, we have the equivalent helper task_pid_nr(). My approach would've been to have Task::pid() wrap around task_pid_nr() (possibly through a helper since I don't see it in the bindings) but maybe that isn't the right approach for what we're doing here? Obviously, I don't think task_struct's pid location is changing soon but I wanted to know which situations should we bring in some of the implementation details to the Rust side?

Milan said:

In the C version of the module, I've used the related functions in_task(), current_uid(), current_euid(), etc. I don't see these in the generated bindings so is my approach in adding wrapper functions for these in helpers.c the right one?

These are C macros, which means bindgen cannot create a Rust equivalent automatically. In these cases, yes, usually you need to create a wrapper function in C that "expands" the macro for you.

Milan said:

On a more broader pattern, I'm struggling to know when to defer to writing a helper that wraps the C equivalent (and therefore shades us from the implementation) or bringing in the implementation details into the Rust side. As a simple example, we currently, in Task::pid() look at a task_struct's pid field. On the C side, we have the equivalent helper task_pid_nr(). My approach would've been to have Task::pid() wrap around task_pid_nr() (possibly through a helper since I don't see it in the bindings) but maybe that isn't the right approach for what we're doing here? Obviously, I don't think task_struct's pid location is changing soon but I wanted to know which situations should we bring in some of the implementation details to the Rust side?

In general, it is best to avoid copying implementation details so that we don't have to keep the two sides in sync. So Task::pid() could definitely have used the macro.

On the other hand, for something as simple as that (and that, as you mention, is unlikely to change), it means we would need to maintain the wrapper which is more complex to follow. And without cross-language LTO, slower too etc.
(task_pid_nr() is not a macro but a static inline, but at least for now, the same applies)
Thanks Miguel! This was helpful
You're welcome!

Another question:
If I am unloading a kernel module that has a static variable, when will its detor be run?? Before KernelModule::Drop::drop() or after it?? Is it deterministic??
Static variables are never dropped in rust.

bjorn3 said:

Static variables are never dropped in rust.

So when we unload module, it is hiding but still occupying the mem?

Is this the same behavior for kernel module written in C??

This is always confusing for me. I saw a speech from Rob Landley before (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Sk9TatW9ino&t=2470s) where he claimed "it will hide from you but not free up the memory" which is like what you say.
But another very very senior kernel developer told me "it removes all links to the code from memory so as far as the kernel knows, it is gone. The memory is free to be overwritten then by anything else that might need it." -- That means it will be destructed when unloading.

Why are static variables needed in a Rust driver in the first place? Do you want to translate line-by-line-ish a driver? My feeling is a lot of static variables in C drivers should be encapsulated in a context structure, if not for the reason that accessing static variables is unsafe in Rust.

Yes, I think adding a method to CStr would make sense. We need to pick an appropriate name though: as_str implies (by convention) that it's free, which it isn't because it needs to check the string is utf-8 compatible, which is an O(n) operation. to_str isn't great because this conversion is fallible, so perhaps we should implement TryFrom (and get TryInto for free)? And/or perhaps also add an unsafe as_str_unchecked, which would be free (based on from_utf8_unchecked)? WDYT?

void f(void) __attribute__((section("def")));

}

section.c:4:1: warning: ignoring attribute ‘section ("abc")’ because it conflicts with previous ‘section ("def")’ [-Wattributes]
    4 | {

    1 | void f(void) __attribute__((section("def")));

// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0

//! Reset controller abstractions.
//!
//! C header: [`include/linux/reset.h`](../../../../include/linux/reset.h)

use core::ffi::c_char;
use crate::{
    bindings,
    error::code::*,
    Result,
    to_result,
};

/// Represents `struct reset_control *`.
///
/// # Invariants
///
/// The pointer is valid.
pub struct Reset(*mut bindings::reset_control);

impl Reset {
    /// Creates a new reset_control structure from a raw pointer.
    ///
    /// # Safety
    ///

@Nikos Maragkos On soundness, raw pointers in safe public APIs is always something to be cautious about: id can be given any value by callers (which includes invalid pointers). Invalid pointers are fine on their own, as long as they are not dereferenced. But the pointer gets passed to the C side, and in C callers are usually trusted on that matter. Thus, the question is: if you get passed a random number as a pointer, will the abstraction break? If yes, then to make things sound you will need to make something unsafe or change the design (e.g. take a CStr instead).
